content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


July 7, 1988


Mr. James Sills
Chief of Staff
Councilman Bruce Henderson
City of San Diego
202 C Street
San Diego, California 92101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-88-230

Dear Mr. Sills:

We have received your letter concerning the new
restrictions on publicly-funded mass mailings under Proposition
73. We appreciate your comments on this subject.

Enclosed are copies of our first advice letters
interpreting the mass mailing restrictions. After examining
the new statute and the analysis by the Legislative Analyst, we
have concluded that the voters intended to restrict mailings by
state and local elected officials, but did not intend to ban
publicly-funded mailings entirely. In the future, we expect to
address additional questions concerning the mass mailing
restrictions in Proposition 73, and we will include your name
on our mailing list for information on the subject.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: [illegible]
Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
Advice Letter No. A-88-220


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

THE CITY OF

SAN DIEGO

CITY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING • 202 C STREET • SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
PHONE (619) 236-6616

J. BRUCE HENDERSON
COUNCILMEMBER
SIXTH DISTRICT

June 17, 1988

California Fair Political Practices
Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sirs:

I am writing to request an interpretation on the recently
enacted Proposition 73. The measure provides controls and prohibitions regarding mass mailings by elected officials.

One provision bans use of public funds "to send" mass mailings. Is my interpretation correct in thinking that use of the
words "to send" applies to costs involved in postage, mailing
services, or other delivery related expenses? This provision
does not specifically call out costs involved in the printing or
preparation of mass mailings. Would, therefore, costs involved
in printing of mass mailings remain an appropriate and justifiable expenditure of public funds?

Please contact me at (619) 236-6616 if I may provide additional information.

Sincerely,

[signature]

JAMES SILLS
Chief of Staff
Councilman Bruce Henderson

JS/fl

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 21, 1988

James Sills
Chief of Staff
Councilman Bruce Henderson
City Administration Building
202 C Street
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: 88-230

Dear Mr. Sills:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on June 20, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Robert Leidigh, an attorney in
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

sections.question: Is my interpretation correct in thinking that use of the words "to send" applies to costs involved in postage, mailing services, or other delivery related expenses? This provision does not specifically callout costs involved in the printing or preparation of mass mailings. Would, therefore, costs involved in printing of mass mailings remain an appropriate and justifiable expenditure of public funds?
sections.conclusion: After examining the new statute and the analysis by the Legislative Analyst, we have concluded that the voters intended to restrict mailings by state and local elected officials, but did not intend to ban publicly-funded mailings entirely.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Proposition 73's ban on using public funds "to send" mass mailings applies only to delivery-related costs (postage, mailing services) or whether it also prohibits publicly-funded printing and preparation of mass mailings by elected officials.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Proposition 73 was intended to restrict mass mailings by elected officials but not to ban publicly-funded mailings entirely, indicating a narrower interpretation than a complete prohibition on public funding for all aspects of mass mailings.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters: incoming request (June 17, 1988), acknowledgment letter (June 21, 1988), and final advice response (July 7, 1988). The July 7 letter provides the substantive FPPC conclusion rather than a detailed formal opinion. The document references attached Advice Letter No. A-88-220 but that letter is not included in the provided text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is my interpretation correct in thinking that use of the words "to send" applies to costs involved in postage, mailing services, or other delivery related expenses? This provision does not specifically callout costs involved in the printing or preparation of mass mailings. Would, therefore, costs involved in printing of mass mailings remain an appropriate and justifiable expenditure of public funds?

CONCLUSION: After examining the new statute and the analysis by the Legislative Analyst, we have concluded that the voters intended to restrict mailings by state and local elected officials, but did not intend to ban publicly-funded mailings entirely.

embedding.summary: Councilman Bruce Henderson's Chief of Staff requested FPPC advice on whether Proposition 73's restriction on using public funds "to send" mass mailings applies only to delivery costs or also covers printing and preparation expenses. The FPPC responded that voters intended to restrict mailings by elected officials but did not intend to ban publicly-funded mailings entirely.
